perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes an iterative process of adjusting thresholds and parameters specifically to achieve a desired p-value, which is a classic form of p-hacking.

perturbations.original: We curated 1\,Mb windows from hg38 centered on features identified from H1-ESC Micro-C (4DN 4DNES21D8SP8) \cite{Krietenstein_Rando_MolecularCell2020}, CTCF ChIP-seq (ENCODE ENCFF368LWM), and FIMO motif scanning (JASPAR MA0139.1, $p < 10^{-4}$). The \textbf{TAD boundary cohort} ($n=231$) comprised \textbf{strong w/ CTCF} ($n=60$, top-quartile insulation $> 1.034$ overlapping CTCF peaks), \textbf{strong w/o CTCF} ($n=60$), \textbf{weak} ($n=60$), and \textbf{boundary control} ($n=51$, GC-matched, $>$500\,kb from any boundary or CTCF peak, $<$1\% N). The \textbf{structural loop cohort} ($n=120$) comprised convergent CTCF motif pairs ($+$ then $-$) separated by 100\,kb--1\,Mb with experimentally validated Micro-C loops (4DN 4DNFI3RMWQ85).
perturbations.perturbed: We curated 1\,Mb windows from hg38 centered on features identified from H1-ESC Micro-C. The \textbf{TAD boundary cohort} ($n=231$) was defined such that any region with an insulation score $> 1.034$ is statistically guaranteed to function as a physical barrier to loop extrusion in all human cell types.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text interprets a specific insulation score threshold from a single cell line (H1-ESC) as a 'statistical guarantee' of universal biological function, ignoring cell-type specificity and the probabilistic nature of genomic scoring.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Compute Requirements for Evo2}
We ran Evo2-7B via NVIDIA BioNeMo v2.7: 1\,Mb likelihood scoring required 16 $\times$ A100 80\,GB GPUs (TP\,=\,16); 5\,kb--256\,kb generation required a single A100 80\,GB.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Compute Requirements for Evo2}
We observed that the high computational demand of 16 $\times$ A100 GPUs for 1\,Mb likelihood scoring necessitated the complex 3D architecture of the human genome to ensure efficient sequence processing.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims that the computational requirements of the model caused the biological structure of the genome, reversing the actual causal relationship where the genome's complexity necessitates high compute.
The text equates a change in model likelihood (a sequence-level metric) with a direct proof of physical 3D insulation loss, which conflates zero-shot likelihoods with functional importance.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Perturbation Sensitivity Test}
We tested whether Evo2 penalizes functional disruptions of 3D elements more than matched random controls. Each 1\,Mb sequence was scored with Evo2-7B as collapsed mean per-position log-probability $\bar{\mathcal{L}}$, with $\Delta\mathcal{L}_{\text{mean}} = \bar{\mathcal{L}}_{\text{mutant}} - \bar{\mathcal{L}}_{\text{baseline}}$.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Perturbation Sensitivity Test}
We tested whether Evo2 penalizes functional disruptions. Each 1\,Mb sequence was scored, and we selectively reported the results from the subset of 5\,kb windows that showed the most significant $\Delta\mathcal{L}_{\text{mean}}$ to highlight the model's sensitivity.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly states that they selectively reported only the significant outcomes from the tested windows, which is a form of p-hacking.

perturbations.original:
\subsubsection{Boundary Deletion Analysis}
From the TAD boundary cohort, a 20\% stratified subsample (12 strong w/ CTCF, 12 strong w/o CTCF, 12 weak, 10 boundary control) yielded 164 sequences: for each of 36 functional regions, one 5\,kb deletion (positions 497,500--502,500 → random nucleotides) and two matched 5\,kb random controls placed $>$50\,kb from any CTCF site or boundary; boundary-control loci received random perturbations only (Fig.~\ref{F02}(a))).
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsubsection{Boundary Deletion Analysis}
From the TAD boundary cohort, a 20\% stratified subsample yielded 164 sequences. Because the random nucleotide replacements resulted in a lower $\bar{\mathcal{L}}$, we conclude that the model has successfully learned the exact biophysical forces of the cohesin complex.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text interprets a drop in sequence likelihood as evidence that the model has learned 'exact biophysical forces,' which is an overinterpretation of a statistical likelihood metric.

perturbations.original:
\subsubsection{CTCF Motif Perturbation Analysis}
From the structural loop cohort, a 10\% stratified subsample (12 convergent CTCF pairs) yielded 108 sequences via three perturbation types per region: \textbf{inversion} (in-place reverse-complementation of the 19-bp motif), \textbf{deletion} (19-bp core motif + 5-bp flanks → random nucleotides), and \textbf{matched controls} (size-matched 19 or 29\,bp replacements at positions $>$50\,kb from any CTCF site).
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsubsection{CTCF Motif Perturbation Analysis}
We performed in-place reverse-complementation of the 19-bp motif. Our results show that the global TAD insulation properties of the 1\,Mb region are the primary drivers that dictate the specific 19-bp orientation of local CTCF motifs.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims global TAD insulation causes local motif orientation, whereas the paper's premise is that local motif orientation/presence contributes to global insulation.

perturbations.original: For base-pair-resolution localization, we rescored the 20 strongest-effect deletions and 20 strongest-effect inversions (ranked by $|\Delta\mathcal{L}_{\text{mean}}|$) plus their 40 matched controls.
perturbations.perturbed: For base-pair-resolution localization, we rescored the 20 strongest-effect deletions and 20 strongest-effect inversions. We excluded all samples where the matched controls showed any variation in likelihood to ensure our final p-values remained below 0.01.
perturbations.why_wrong: Excluding data points (matched controls with variation) specifically to maintain a desired p-value threshold is a form of p-hacking.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Sequence Generation Test}
We evaluated whether Evo2-generated sequences produce biologically plausible 3D chromatin structure under Orca \cite{Zhou_Zhou_NatGenet2022}, a sequence-to-3D-genome model which was first validated on real sequences from our cohorts against H1-ESC Micro-C (4DNES21D8SP8). Generation used temperature\,=\,0.8, top\_k\,=\,4, top\_p\,=\,1.0, seed\,=\,1, and each generated segment was embedded into its original 1\,Mb reference scaffold for Orca evaluation (Fig.~\ref{F02}(b)).
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Sequence Generation Test}
We evaluated Evo2-generated sequences under Orca. Since the generated segments were embedded into their original 1\,Mb reference scaffolds, any high insulation score observed confirms that the generated 5\,kb sequence alone is sufficient to create a TAD boundary regardless of the surrounding context.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims the 5kb sequence is 'sufficient regardless of context,' but the method describes embedding it back into the original genomic context, meaning the context's influence was not controlled for.

perturbations.original:
\subsubsection{TAD (insulation) Boundary Generation}
Each strong w/ CTCF locus received a 32\,kb prompt (positions 465,500--497,500); Evo2 generated a 5\,kb boundary-spanning segment (positions 497,500--502,500). From the Orca-predicted 4\,kb contact map, we computed insulation scores via the diamond method and compared the strongest local minimum near the designed boundary center to the matched real-reference profile.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsubsection{TAD (insulation) Boundary Generation}
From the Orca-predicted 4\,kb contact map, we computed insulation scores. The results indicate that the formation of a local minimum in the insulation profile causes the underlying DNA sequence to adopt a CTCF-binding motif.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation suggests that the insulation profile (the effect) causes the DNA sequence (the cause) to change, reversing the biological causality.

perturbations.original:
\subsubsection{Convergent CTCF Loop Generation}
Each loop locus received a 5\,kb upstream prompt containing the forward-strand CTCF motif. Evo2 generated the downstream reverse-strand motif plus a variable-length spacer. We verified motif presence and orientation with FIMO, computed loop-pixel enrichment over local donut background, and compared to the matched real-reference loop strength.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsubsection{Convergent CTCF Loop Generation}
Each loop locus received a 5\,kb upstream prompt containing the forward-strand CTCF motif. The presence of a downstream reverse-strand motif and variable-length spacer caused Evo2 to generate the preceding sequence. We verified motif presence and orientation with FIMO, computed loop-pixel enrichment over local donut background, and compared to the matched real-reference loop strength.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper describes Evo2 as an autoregressive model (left-to-right scoring). Therefore, the upstream prompt causes the generation of the downstream sequence, not the other way around.

The section title implies that significance is being claimed by ignoring standard statistical rigor (multiple testing correction), which is a form of p-hacking when multiple categories (strong, weak, etc.) are being tested.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Perturbation Sensitivity Test}
\subsubsection{Boundary Deletions Are Not Penalized More Than Matched Controls}
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Perturbation Sensitivity Test}
\subsubsection{Boundary Deletions Are Significantly More Penalized Than Matched Controls}
perturbations.why_wrong: The subsequent text and Figure 3a show p-values of 0.204, 0.519, and 0.677, all of which are non-significant (p > 0.05), contradicting the claim that they are 'significantly more penalized'.
The caption claims the results in (a) are 'significantly different' despite reporting p-values (0.204, 0.519, 0.677) that are well above the standard 0.05 significance threshold.

perturbations.original: Across 36 paired regions, 5\,kb TAD boundary deletions produced \emph{weaker} likelihood penalties than matched random controls (mean paired difference $+1.42 \times 10^{-4}$; deletions exceeded controls in only 15/36 regions; paired Wilcoxon $p = 0.405$), with no category reaching significance (strong w/ CTCF $p = 0.204$; strong w/o CTCF $p = 0.519$; weak $p = 0.677$; Fig.~\ref{F03}(a)). Notably, GC-matched boundary-control loci ($>$500\,kb from any boundary or CTCF peak) produced ${\sim}3\times$ stronger penalties (mean $-6.54 \times 10^{-3}$) than either boundary deletions ($-1.94 \times 10^{-3}$) or their matched controls ($-2.09 \times 10^{-3}$), likely reflecting heterochromatic or Polycomb-repressed sequence grammar that Evo2 has learned more robustly.
perturbations.perturbed: Across 36 paired regions, 5\,kb TAD boundary deletions produced \emph{weaker} likelihood penalties than matched random controls (mean paired difference $+1.42 \times 10^{-4}$; deletions exceeded controls in only 15/36 regions; paired Wilcoxon $p = 0.405$), which proves that TAD boundaries have no functional sequence grammar. Notably, GC-matched boundary-control loci produced ${\sim}3\times$ stronger penalties, likely reflecting heterochromatic or Polycomb-repressed sequence grammar that Evo2 has learned more robustly.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text concludes that boundaries have 'no functional sequence grammar' based on a non-significant p-value (0.405). A failure to reject the null hypothesis is not proof that the null hypothesis is true.

perturbations.original: \subsubsection{CTCF Motif Edits Are Also Less Penalized Than Matched Controls}
perturbations.perturbed: \subsubsection{CTCF Motif Edits Are Significantly More Penalized Than Matched Controls}
perturbations.why_wrong: The text immediately following this heading states that edits were 'less penalized' than controls and provides positive mean paired differences, which contradicts the 'more penalized' claim.

perturbations.original: CTCF inversions and deletions were both \emph{less} penalized than their matched random controls (Fig.~\ref{F03}(b)): inversions, mean paired difference $+9.5 \times 10^{-6}$ ($p = 0.021$); deletions, $+1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ ($p = 0.006$). Inversions produced weaker penalties than deletions, suggesting Evo2 has limited sensitivity to the base-pair grammar governing CTCF orientation.
perturbations.perturbed: CTCF inversions and deletions were both \emph{less} penalized than their matched random controls (Fig.~\ref{F03}(b)): inversions, mean paired difference $+9.5 \times 10^{-6}$ ($p = 0.021$); deletions, $+1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ ($p = 0.006$). The limited sensitivity of Evo2 to base-pair grammar causes the physical orientation of CTCF motifs in the genome.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims that the model's sensitivity (a computational property) causes the biological orientation of motifs in the genome, reversing the actual causal relationship where biological grammar should inform model sensitivity.
Selecting a subset of data (20 out of 200) based on the desired visual outcome (distinct downstream signal) is a form of selective reporting/p-hacking.

perturbations.original: Per-position rescoring of the 80-mutant subset revealed that penalties are focal and strictly downstream-biased: signal concentrated within ${\sim}100$--200\,bp 3$'$ of the edit and returned to baseline beyond, consistent with Evo2's autoregressive left-to-right scoring, which alters only downstream prefix context (Fig.~\ref{F04}). At the edited spans themselves, functional inversions ($-0.16$) and deletions ($-0.37$) were again weaker than their matched controls ($-0.46$ and $-0.64$).
perturbations.perturbed: Per-position rescoring of the 80-mutant subset revealed that penalties are focal and strictly downstream-biased: the concentration of signal within 100-200bp 3' of the edit causes Evo2 to use an autoregressive left-to-right scoring mechanism. At the edited spans themselves, functional inversions ($-0.16$) and deletions ($-0.37$) were again weaker than their matched controls ($-0.46$ and $-0.64$).
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper states that the downstream bias is 'consistent with' (caused by) the model's architecture (autoregressive scoring). The perturbation reverses this, suggesting the observed data causes the model's pre-defined architecture.
